flashcards.front: What defines a Circular Singly Linked List?
flashcards.back: A linked list where the last node points back to the first node instead of containing a NULL pointer, forming a closed loop.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of using a circular linked list?
flashcards.back: Efficient continuous traversal from any node and ideal for round-robin applications where processing needs to cycle through elements repeatedly.

flashcards.front: How do you detect the end of a circular linked list during traversal?
flashcards.back: You don't have a traditional end. Traversal continues until you return to the starting node. Use a do-while loop that continues while current != starting_node.

flashcards.front: What special case must be handled when deleting from a circular linked list?
flashcards.back: When deleting the only node in the list, you must set the head pointer to NULL and ensure the node doesn't point to itself anymore.

flashcards.front: Why is a circular linked list suitable for implementing a queue?
flashcards.back: It allows efficient enqueue and dequeue operations with O(1) complexity if you maintain a rear pointer, as front can be accessed as rear->next.
